input.question: We are a student engineering team designing a vehicle for the FSAE competition. Attached is the FSAE rules document. Also attached is an image that shows an engineering drawing of the vehicle on the top accompanied by six CAD views of the vehicle on the bottom. The six CAD views each feature a different orientation of our design, so that 3D information about our design can be inferred. In the CAD views, the relevant aerodynamic devices are highlighted in pink. The CAD views are provided to contextualize the engineering drawing, which has the same orientation as one of the six CAD views. All units displayed in the engineering drawing have units of mm. Based on the engineering drawing, does our design comply with rule T.7.6.3 (which refers to T.7.3.3) specified in the FSAE rule document? To answer the question, use the scale bar shown at the top of the engineering drawing to compute necessary dimensions in the drawing.  First provide an explanation for your answer (begin it with 'Explanation:'). Then provide just a yes/no answer (begin it with 'Answer:') that summarizes your response.

retrieved_context:
RULE: t.7.6.3
            TITLE: when rearward of the centerline of the rear wheel axles:

            CONTENT:
            rule T.7.6.3: When rearward of the centerline of the rear wheel axles:
T.7.6.3 When rearward of the centerline of the rear wheel axles: 
In the Rear Aerodynamic Zone

RULE: t.7.3.3
            TITLE: rear aerodynamic zone

            CONTENT:
            rule T.7.3.3: Rear Aerodynamic Zone
T.7.3.3 Rear Aerodynamic Zone 
The volume that is: 
• Rearward of the Head Restraint Plane 
• Inboard of two vertical planes parallel to the centerline of the chassis touching the inside 
of the rear tires at the height of the hub centerline

final_answer:
Explanation:The compliance check is based on Rule T.7.6.3, which references Rule T.7.3.3, defining the Rear Aerodynamic Zone. According to T.7.3.3, this zone is the volume that is rearward of the Head Restraint Plane and inboard of two vertical planes parallel to the centerline of the chassis touching the inside of the rear tires at the height of the hub centerline. The attached CAD views highlight the aerodynamic bodywork in pink. Visually inspecting the bodywork across all views (Top, Front, Side), the pink components appear to be contained within the general envelope defined by the rear wheel track and extend rearward from the chassis. While precise measurements for the Head Restraint Plane are unavailable, the visible bodywork does not appear to violate the lateral or longitudinal boundaries established by the rear tires and the general rearward extension required by the rule. Therefore, based on the provided visual evidence, the design appears to comply with the definition of the Rear Aerodynamic Zone.
Answer: yes
